tags: Festival of New Music, Book Arsenal
performers: Emanuele Torquati, piano, tape, video projection
program: Lepo Sumera — Two pieces from the year 1981: n. 1 (1981)
Michel van der Aa — Transit (2009) for piano, electronics and video projection

All works receive their national premiere, and the concert marks Emanuele Torquati’s first performance in Ukraine.
description: Transit is a solo Piano Recital by Italian pianist Emanuele Torquati, which is intended to bring the audience in different soundscapes and styles. In the first part, two works from Estonian composer Lepo Sumera and American living legend Terry Riley will show different paths for Minimalism. Between these two, it will be presented Michel Van der Aa’s last piano piece, based on a video made by the composer himself in New York.

The second part presents works of Italian composers belonging to different generations, a virtuosic Etude by Ivan Fedele and the ironic and full of “humour” Filastrocca by rising talent Francesco Filidei, coupled with a piece written by one of most interesting voices among young American composers, Ryan Carter. The program ends with a masterwork devoted to Olivier Messiaen by Jonathan Harvey, one of the central figures in new music world, recently passed away.

name: Kronos Quartet Arrives in Kyiv
program: Bryce Dessner — Aheym (2009) for string quartet
Nicole Lizée — Death to Kosmische (2010) for string quartet, vintage electronics (Stylophone, Omnichord), and track
Omar Souleyman (arr. Jacod Garchik) — La Sidounak Sayyada (I’ll Prevent the Hunters from Hunting You)
Ram Narayan (arr. Kronos) — Raga Mishra Bhairavi: Alap
Valentyn Silvestrov — String Quartet No. 3 (2011)
Steve Reich — WTC 9/11 (2010) for string quartet and pre-recorded audio
Mariana Sadovska — Chornobyl. The Harvest (2013) for voice, harmoinum, and string quartet. World premiere
description: Each of us in Kronos is delighted to be making our debut in Kyiv. Kyiv has one of the most influential musical cultures of any city in the world. The history of our instruments has been often shaped and defined by the personalities, sounds and the souls of Ukrainian virtuosos. John, Hank, Sunny and I hope to honor this history.

It is with great pride that we are able to bring home music by two wonderful Ukrainian composers: Valentin Silvestrov and Mariana Sadovska. For many years Valentin Silvestrov has fearlessly forged new paths. He is a rare and singular musical poet whose inner voice and muse is directly connected to the depths of European music and its treasured history. His String Quartet No. 3 has given our work a new palette to explore infinitely. I hope that his next piece for Kronos will be a Piano Quintet that we might play together with him.

Mariana Sadovska's Chernobyl. The Harvest features a universe of feelings and gives voice and an acknowledgment to a vast environmental and human apocalypse. With her vivid singing voice combined with her ethnomusicological knowledge, her music gives Kronos a direct connection to ancient Ukrainian culture. Mariana's vibrant new work promises much for the future and I hope that Chornobyl. The Harvest is the very beginning of a long and fruitful relationship between us.

Additionally, with our debut in Kiev we will attempt to give our audience a window onto some of the rest of the big world of music Kronos is a part of. We delight in creating contrasts. By joining the propulsive rhythmic drive of Bryce Dessner's electric guitar-influenced Aheym to the vintage electronic pop culture of Nicole Lizee's Death to Kosmische to the wild Syrian wedding music of Omar Souleyman to the repose and inwardness of Ram Narayan's Alap from Raga Mishra Bhairavi to the tragic immediacy of Steve Reich's WTC 9/11, we hope to fuse an insatiable thirst for musical exploration to the minds and hearts of our listeners. And as the element of surprise is one of life's great delights, there may be several of those as well.
David Harrington

name: AoV: Ghédalia
program: Ghédalia Tazartès performs Coda Lunga (2012) in his Ukrainian debut
description: Folklore of nonexistent countries; Mallarmé; chanson; throat singing; lament; scream. Ghédalia Tazartès is an orchestra and a pop group in a single body—an opera dispersing into an endless multiplicity of characters. When the mirage dissolves, what remains is a lone figure at the microphone, a tape recorder in hand. “I’m simply less afraid of my voice than most people,” he says.
The concert by the cult French musician Ghédalia Tazartès opens the Architecture of Voice cycle as its prologue

name: AoV: A Walk with a Choir
place: Underground passage at European Square, continuing to Volodymyrska Hill & Kokorev Gazebo
performers: Students of conservatory and Lysenko music school
program: A one-hour vocal walk performing medieval canons, with singers joining along the way
description: We stretched a vocal walk through the city over ten months; now we will compress it into one hour.
If singing was possible in a greenhouse, it is possible in an open garden as well.

Once, Volodymyrska Hill was the starting point of an annual religious procession. Our micro-procession is of a different kind. It does not move toward a sacred object, and it contains no ritual — yet it is still pilgrim-like, in the sense that we, too, are interested in the possibility of an overlap between an external and an internal journey.

We begin at 9:00 pm in the long underground passage beneath European Square, then walk toward the hill and wander along its lower terrace, stopping from time to time. The core musical material consists of medieval canons; along the way, there will be uneven skirmishes with Trukhaniv Island discos, unpleasant smells, the rumble of cobblestones, and the occasional cries of the city’s madmen.

Walking with us are the Alter Ratio Vocal Ensemble, the Dnipro Choir, and students of the Tchaikovsky National Music Academy of Ukraine and the Kyiv State Music Lyceum named after Mykola Lysenko. The number of singers will change; at its peak, there will be fifty voices.
You may follow the entire route, or you may encounter us at one of the points in the park. There is only one request: listen.
The architecture of voice is departing — though there will be an epilogue.

Olivier Messiaen – Oiseaux exotiques (05.10.1955-03.01.1956) for solo piano and small orchestra,  16’
Toshio Hosokawa – Drawing (2004) for eight musicians, 16’
subtitle: Ukho Ensemble Kyiv: Debut Concert
description: The opening concert of the festival has been in preparation since that day in June when we announced auditions for the Ukho Ensemble. We reviewed over one hundred and fifty applications, auditioned sixty musicians, selected seventeen for the core group and another eight for the extended team.

What does one need to know about the ensemble? First, that there will be no repertoire compromises. The music the ensemble will offer largely falls within complex contemporary classical. However, Ukho’s hope is that our musicians approach it in such a way that, in the listeners’ experience, this complexity turns into rapture rather than painful boredom. How exactly do we plan to achieve this? Through the most careful selection of pieces, backbreaking rehearsals, and the shared interpretative labour of the conductor and musicians.

The conductor is Luigi Gaggero, a cimbalomist and a poet. His first piece may seem like a quatrain, but in truth there is an extra, silent line: one could argue that the composers in the program are five, not four, since Messiaen, Grisey, Takemitsu, and Hosokawa all rest on the impressionist shoulders of Claude Debussy.

The next paternal figure in line is Olivier Messiaen. An organist, an ornithologist, and one of the key visionaries we will be speaking of throughout this festival series, he claimed, among other things, that music does not divide into tonal and serial; rather, there is music of color and music that lacks color. Without founding a school or movement of his own, he influenced several generations of composers: beyond Boulez and Grisey, his direct students, this includes Takemitsu and Hosokawa. Messiaen himself, in turn, was deeply taken with traditional Japanese music, another rhyme within the program.

The concert begins with the Ukrainian premiere of Gérard Grisey’s Partiels. Its opening is one of the defining moments in spectral music: an orchestrated unfolding of the overtone spectrum of a low E, modelled from a sonogram of a trombone attack and distributed across instruments according to the dynamic evolution of each partial. Come and hear this orchestrally synthesized spectrum.

name: Hommage à Pierre Boulez
program: Alexey Retinsky – Долго (и в той долготе - беспрерывно-внезапно) (2015), 7’
Ihor Zavhorodnii – Радості птахів, яких не існує (2015), 7’
Ostap Manulyak – A quick glance at landscape (2015), 7’
Pierre Boulez –  Dérive 1 (1984), 7’
Pierre Boulez – Dérive 2 (1988), 42’
description: Every church needs its Pierre. On the occasion of his 90th anniversary, we have painted the Pierre of new music into pink (poster explained!).

So many things have fit themselves into the life of Pierre Boulez that they come through his biography in constellations of most amazing shapes. Nothing is surprising: neither the fact that being a radical vanguardist, a key Darmstadt composer, he also won twenty six Grammy awards (out of sixty seven nominations) both as conductor and composer; nor a brief dialogue, which took place in the Parisian metro in the end of 40ies, between Boulez and Olivier Messiaen. “But who is to put music in order? It is in such a horrible condition!”, Boulez was asking, – “You!”, his teacher replied.

The program of the concert is merciless: of Boulez it features ‘Dérive 1’ (1984) for six instruments and eight minutes, and ‘Dérive 2’ (1988-2006/2009) for eleven instruments and forty five minutes. The second piece is one of the most complex works in contemporary music and has never been performed in Ukraine before. Some light is shed by its name. ‘Dérive’ translates as deviation or derivative thereof: all the music that one hears in the piece (and this is one of the most dense, elaborative scores out there) is based on one chord. It is a hermetic world, but it also is a world that constantly expands, rewrites itself.  Its creator chooses to control even the smallest, most insignificant detail, professing his will, bordering megalomania. Furthermore, ‘Dérive’ reads as a drift: all changes occur at the surface, inside changes nothing. Tom Service is right in noting that it reminds a moving flock of sardines: a constant transformation of shape and a constant return to the original.

We made a New Year gift for ourselves commissioning three new pieces dedicated to Pierre Boulez. We have commissioned them from four young Ukrainian composers, three have managed to complete their tasks so far: Alexey Retinsky, who resides in Vienna, Ostap Manulyak, who resides in Lviv, and Igor Zavgorodnii, who resides in Kyiv. All of them are to attend the premiere (Igor is to attend it as our violinist).

description: In the video below (we invite you to check it) Luigi speaks of Ligeti and his masterpiece of a Chamber Concerto. Other things we invite you to do before the upcoming concert include finding the infamous video which documents Barbara Hannigan popping a bubblegum and singing “Mysteries of the macabre” in a bold skirt; recalling why the ensemble has been performing Hosokawa at every concert; listening to ‘Aus bebung’ by Michael Jarrell, imagining how this piece would sound if solos of clarinet and cello were framed by ten more instruments. It is also extremely important that you research the tragic figure of Christophe Bertrand.

Words fail us this time, but logistics doesn’t. Therefore we have for you: an ensemble of twenty four musicians, a pair of finest conductor ears, coloratura soprano of Lena Arbuzova, two pianos, a mandolin, a celesta, an electric organ, a harpsichord and a harpsichordist – along with percussions that come in all shapes and sizes. It is very likely that we are also in possession of an oboe d’amore, but with all things d’amore one can never know for sure.

name: Silvestrov: Maidan-2014
program: Valentyn Silvestrov — Maidan 2014 (choral cantata), world premiere
description: Two years and eight days ago, we held a night-long piano marathon at the House of Architects. We prepared for provocations and hired thugs, assuming the audience might not come — but 500 people did. In the end, the real problem was the bass from the nightclub next door thudding through the wall.
The concert hall was guarded by members of the Maidan Self-Defense units. What became of them later — two years and six, five, four days ago — we do not know. We can only hope they made it.
This performance is about them, about us, about everyone who lived through Kyiv’s winter of 2014. It is also about those who proved too brave, too unyielding for it — those it could not swallow.
In June, at “Silent Songs” in Tarilka, we promised Valentyn Silvestrov that we would present his Maidan cycle; he very much wanted this music to be heard now.
February 27, 7:00 pm
House of Architects
Maidan 2014, choral cantata

name: Viaggio in Italia
program: Carmine Emanuele Cella  – La mémoire de l’eau (2014) for nine musicians, 10’
Luca Francesconi – Secondo concerto per oboe (1991), 16’
Niccolò Castiglioni – Momenti musicali (1992) for seven instruments, 9’
Salvatore Sciarrino – Vento d’ombra (2005) for thirteen musicians, 10’
Franco Donatoni – Hot (1989) for solo saxophone and six musicians, 15’
description: What does one know about the city of Palermo?

930 km to Rome, 370 km to Tunis, as the bird flies. Mimosa blossoms line the roads into town; eight thousand mummies rest in the Capuchin Catacombs—the last body was mummified in 1920. They stand, sit, lie, hang on walls and from ceilings;  they form compositions. All of which can be viewed for a three-euro entrance ticket.

Ceilings are abundant in Palermo. Think stalactites of the Palatine Chapel, carved of the finest oak by Arabian carpenters on the orders of King Roger II. Think Persian gold mosaics of the Kings’ own chambers.

Lying beneath Roger’s Castle, just as they were a thousand years ago, are slums. The peculiarity of Palermitan slums is that they cut into the body of elegant quarters without prior notice. There’s a mansion circled by a line of tangerine trees and there’s a rotten shack around the corner; a group of well-dressed school children passes by.

In 1978 a thirty year old Peppino Impastato, the first political activist in Sicily to publicly attack the mafia, was blown up in Cinisi, a small town on the outskirts of Palermo. It took more than twenty years to bring his murderers to trial. Salvatore Sciarrino, born in Palermo and a year older than Impastato, was already living in Milan in 1978. Sciarrino speaks softly, but his whisper is instantly recognizable. In Vento d’ombra (Wind from the Shadow), one cannot quite discern which shadow the composer whispers of, though it is unlikely to be a summer breeze.
What does one know about the city of Milan?

Franco Donatoni was born there in 1927, Niccolò Castiglioni in 1932, and Luca Francesconi in 1956. Carmine Emanuele Cella, by contrast, was born in Urbino in 1976. Three generations of Italian composers appear in a single program, and a few mummies on top.

name: Ukho Comes to 86: Sounds from Dangerous Places & Other Sounds
tags: 86 at Slavutych: Slavutych Stadium, Slavutych Train Station
place: Peter Cusack, audio installations & solo guitar performance
performers: Audio installations from Sounds from Dangerous Places and related field recordings, culminating in a solo performance by Peter Cusack
program: Peter Cusack is a guitarist, an improviser, and a distinguished veteran of the European avant-garde. These days he calls himself a sound journalist, and his collages “sounding portraits” of places and situations: ice cracking on Lake Baikal; an elderly poet in Chernobyl reading aloud; oil pumps groaning in Azerbaijan; children shouting in London schoolyards; pensioners arguing politics; toy instruments still chiming in an abandoned kindergarten.

Cusack insists that his works are not noise music but sonic reportage: there is more documentary than art in them. For the listener, however, this “journey” is condensed — dozens of hours of recordings may become a minute of mixtape; the audio landscape shifts at will.

A documentary film festival seems well suited to documentary music. An ideal city project — Slavutych, all fountains and no water, bus stops and no buses — could use a soundtrack. On May 7, we invite you to wander through Slavutych’s railway station, stadium, and main square to test Cusack’s audio installations (all day, based on Sounds from Dangerous Places) and his guitar (5:30–6:30 pm, in a concert-walk).
A sidenote: Cusack’s guitar was first put to the test by Fred Frith in 1974 on the album Guitar Solos; later, members of the quartet Alterations continued the experiment for years.

All you need to do is come to Slavutych. Entry to everything, including the films of the impeccably curated 86 programme, is free. Follow the announcements; Slavutych is small, we won’t lose each other.

Toru Takemitsu – Rain Coming (1982), 10’
Elliott Carter  – Canaries  (1950) for four timpani, 3’
Edgard Varèse – Intégrales (1923-1925) for eleven wind instruments and four percussionists, 11’
Toshio Hosokawa – Voyage V (2001) for flute and chamber orchestra, 17’
description: On May 25th Ukho Ensemble will take the stage of Kyiv Philharmonics together with Mario Caroli, one of the greatest living flutists. To celebrate the occasion, we have inventively coded the program into visuals of the poster. An attentive look will spot:
the organized sound masses and the crystallization of sound (Intégrales and Octandre, Edgard Varèse),
the circulation of tonality in nature (Rain Coming, Toru Takemitsu),
a song thrown into the universe, to which universe responds  (Voyage V, Toshio Hosokawa), and last but not the least,
a preposterous desire to wrap up (Coda, Pascal Dusapin).
Coded outside the poster are timpani solo in the works of Elliot Carter, performed by Luigi Gaggero.

Ornate plaster moldings, taffeta curtains, and softest velvet seats of Philharmonics make a stark contrast to the 1.5 km2 of unheated halls of Plivka, where clouds of dust are both damp and eternal. Planting a chamber ensemble into a construction site has been quite an adventure, but we are truly taken by the perspectives of cross-disciplinary collaboration Plivka can offer us – so velvet shall be rare.

Adventure’s first season is now approaching its end. It featured over two hundred rehearsal hours, scores arriving from Berlin and Tokyo, rare instruments arriving from Genoa and Paris, musicians of rare talent arriving from just about everywhere. Who can know what lies ahead?

name: Kazuki Tomokawa in Kyiv
program: Kazuki Tomokawa, a seminal figure of Japanese acid folk, makes his first appearance in Ukraine
description: Kazuki Tomokawa is a poet, painter, cycling commentator, actor, essayist and drinker. Some call him "the patriarch of Japanese acid folk", some "the screaming philosopher.”  Songs thick with language, a cracked voice, minutes of trance and near-madness — Tomokawa contains all of this.

In 1993 he released Hanabana no Kashitsu (“Fault of Flowers”) on the cult Japanese label P.S.F. Records, a Tokyo-based imprint devoted to underground psychedelic, folk, and improvised music that has championed figures from Acid Mothers Temple to Keiji Haino. Since then, most of Tomokawa’s albums, both new and reissued, have appeared on P.S.F. He has also contributed music to films by Takashi Miike and Kōji Wakamatsu, and appears on screen in Miike’s Izo (2004).

Tomokawa is Brel, Agujetas, and Cohen, but out of Akita. His concert marks one of Ukho’s most radical departures from the academic canon — and serves as the pilot for a new series launching this autumn at Plivka.

name: AoV, Vol.2: Around Stadia
program: A butoh-opera with improvising musicians, inspired by Pasolini’s Medea, set against the roof of NSC Olimpiyskiy Stadium
subtitle: Medea: a butoh opera
description: In his great Medea (1969), Pasolini dissects mythological consciousness and stages the eternal domestic drama of jealousy and betrayal. The solitary figure of the Colchian sorceress stands like a tree struck by the lightning of fate. In the entire film she weeps only once — unlike Euripides’ heroine, who never stops crying. Maria Callas achieves her effect through micro-movements: statuesque, sparing in expression, as if she alone holds the right to give life and to take it away. The settings are deliberately unstable: the Golden Fleece framed by half-erased Byzantine frescoes; the women of Corinth in pleated dresses; Medea’s pagan temple set within the ruins of an early Christian basilica. Archetype does not require plausibility in detail.

Our Medea begins there — but only there. It is a work for five improvising musicians, two butoh choreographers, and thirty dancers who took part in a two-week workshop, directed by Valentin Tszin and Flavia Ghisalberti. It bears almost none of the formal traits of opera. There is no libretto: the story is preverbal, unfolding through shifts in the heroine’s emotional states: from ritual concentration to boundless erotic rapture, from restraint and fragile hope to paralyzing emptiness. Medea herself remains nearly motionless; little of what happens within her surfaces in movement. The musical ensemble is an echo of her past, the world she abandons for Jason. The butoh ensemble represents the external world and the chaos produced by its clash with Medea’s own reality.
The performance unfolds in the futuristic exterior of the Olympic Stadium, with scenography by Katya Libkind.
We may be inviting you to the cinema. For us, this staging has been a singular experience, we believe it may prove so for you as well.

subtitle: Rothko Chapel
description: On June 16th we are putting together a Ukrainian premiere of three pieces by Morton Feldman – ‘Voices & Instruments 1-2’ and the legendary ‘Rothko Chapel’, performed by the Alter Ratio Vocal Ensemble and Ukho Ensemble under the baton of Luigi Gaggero.

Before becoming ‘Start’, the closest stadium to Kyiv’s Jewish district was named ‘Zenith’. When Nazi troops entered Kyiv in September 1941 the stadium and the army quarter’s next to it were made into a military prisoners’ camp. On September 29th and 30th, 1941, thousands of Kyiv Jews marched past the stadium on their way to Babyn Yar. On October 1st, 1941, three hundred prisoners of war were taken from the camp to Babyn Yar, with shovels, to bury the dead.

In 1942 the stadium was returned to the football team of Kyiv’s bread factory. The team was called ‘Start’ and engaged a group of players from the then-disbanded Dynamo Kyiv, hence the premises very soon became the focal point of football in the occupied city. In a series of games that took place in the summer of 1942, the Kyivite ‘Start’ team defeated the teams of Hungarian troops, German artillery, German railroad, and finally, twice, a team of German air-defense officers. The last game with those became known as a half-mythical “death match”, as within several weeks most of the locals partaking in it were arrested or killed.
After the war and until 1981 the stadium, now known as ‘Start’, was called ‘Avantgarde’.

Mark Rothko, the classic of American abstractionism, was a son of Yakov Rotkovich from Dvinsk. His friend, Morton Feldman, a classic of contemporary American music, was the son of a tailor from Kyiv.  Our listeners are well aware that the music of Feldman engenders “vast, quiet, and agonizingly beautiful worlds of sound” – in this concert, we mean it as a sound memorial.

description: Surprise! We have kept quiet for a while, but the summer break hasn’t begun just yet. Starting Monday, a small international group of students will partake in our conducting masterclass, working with Luigi and the ensemble for a week. They will study some of the most compelling pieces we performed this year: Ligeti’s Chamber Concerto, Hosokawa’s Drawing,  Boulez’s Dérive 1, and Intégrales by Varèse. The outcome will be presented in a gala concert, where Juilliard’s graduate Rachel Koblyakov — who is to join us permanently next season — will play first violin.

name: Onset: Contribution 1
program: Edgard Varèse – Ionisation (1929–1931), 6‘
Luciano Berio – Chemins II (1967), 13’
György Ligeti – Nouvelles Aventures (1962-1965), 13’
Christophe Bertrand – Scales (2008-2009), 20’
Carmine Emanuele Cella – Upside-Down (2004), 10’
description: The season opens! With a concert of Ukho Ensemble on October 1st, and a solo performance by pianist Mark Knoop on October 2nd. Both are part of ONSET, a multi-hour and multi-voice presentation of Plivka Art Center.
What do we offer you on October 1st?

To start, Edgar Varese's “Ionization”, the first significant piece written for a percussion ensemble in the history of classical music. Thirteen percussionists (we called upon all of Kyiv’s orchestras) and thirty-six percussion instruments are used to delineate, in a retro-futuristic manner, a person’s inner world through an extremely artificial, “industrial” sound – which thus becomes external. Fit within Ukho’s overarching program, this composition is intended to balance the naturalist sound of Sciarrino’s work presented in spring.

Second is Berio’s “Chemins II”, a notoriously difficult piece of viola repertoire. Our violist, Natalia Onishchuk, has been preparing it since last November. In it Berio manages to be both a full-fledged colleague of the structuralists Boulez and Stockhausen and, in a sense, a baroque author, as he tends to develop the same idea of ​​virtuosity as, say, Vivaldi.

Next is György Ligeti with his almost theatrical “Nouvelles Aventures”, performed by the soloists of Alter Ratio Vocal Ensemble, followed by Christophe Bertrand’s “Scales" for twenty six instruments. To clarify some ambiguities in the score of the latter, Luigi met with Bertrand's father in Paris and borrowed the original manuscript from him. A work about speed and coherence, it has phases like “come pazzo” or “aggredire il pubblico” among the dynamics instructions. The flip side of this hyperdrive is the sensation that the composer cannot let himself fall silent for a split-second, as even slow fragments are played in fortissimo.

Bertrand wrote “Scales” in 2008–2009 at the age of 27, one year before his death. Carmine Cella was even younger when he composed “Upside-Down” for piano and chamber orchestra in 2004, a fresh, rhythmic, and vivacious piece. At the time Cella listened to a lot of jazz, hence the opening quote from Keith Jarrett. It is a premiere performance, and to ensure we do it justice, we have invited Mark Knoop as a soloist. Mark is one of the most elegant pianists Ukho has hosted, and a piano aid of the London-based Apartment House – naturally, we couldn’t let him leave without a solo recital, please bear with us on October 2nd.

name: György Kurtág 90
program: György Kurtág
Hommage à Berényi Ferenc 70 (1997) for cimbalom, 2’
Brefs messages, op. 47 (2011) for nine instruments,  8’
…quasi una fantasia... op. 27/1 (1987–1988) for piano and ensemble, 9’
Songs to poems by Anna Akhmatova, op. 41 (1997– in progress) for soprano and ensemble, 15’
Tre pezzi, op. 38 (1996) for clarinet and cimbalom, 3’
Messages of the Late Miss R.V. Troussova, Op. 17 (1976–1980) for soprano and ensemble, 26’
description: This year György Kurtág—who is almost entirely unknown in Ukraine—turns 90. Perhaps, the greatest living composer, he still writes and  performs concerts as a pianist. Ukho’s fifth season is built around micro-series, and as an extension of the Hungarian line from last year our next sequence is on Kurtág. It features three concerts and one open rehearsal, in which almost all pieces will be performed in Kyiv for the first time.

One of the key ideas for Kurtág is that each movement of the performer should be internally justified, deserved. Truly playing crescendo or sforzato is only possible if you invest yourself as if it was a matter of your survival. The simpler the material is, the more demanding it is. Put by Kurtág, “my bitterest foe is a musician who hurries to play the next measure not having earned it”.

The program of this concert includes ‘...quasi una fantasia…' for piano and orchestra, ’Brefs messages’ for orchestra, several solo pieces for cimbalom, ‘Songs to Poems by Anna Akhmatova’, and ‘Messages of the Late Miss R.V. Trusova’ for soprano and ensemble. The latter piece is one of the most expressive vocal compositions of the second half of the 20th century — a deep musical text concerning the nature of unrequited love. Written between 1976 and 1980, it is reminiscent of Schoenberg's ‘Pierrot Lunaire’ — 21 songs for soprano solo and chamber ensemble. Kurtág put to music the texts of Rimma Dalosh (who abandoned her maiden name, Troussova, after her move to Hungary) — naive, eccentric, somewhat kitsch, and very honest. “Without you, I’m like that woman in a bathhouse, with her breasts cut off”, the singer’s voice advances through the entire spectrum of feelings experienced in love. At times the ensemble has absolutely nothing to answer with, apart from the sound of breaking glass.

Identification with a female figure and reliance on the Russian verse are two key things ‘Troussova' has in common with ‘Songs to Poems by Anna Akhmatova’. Kurtág speaks six languages. He learned Russian in order to read Dostoevsky, and this primary intention apparently has left its mark. For him Russian seems to be the language that suits suffering, intimacy, and borderline experiences more than any other.
And now, to the fun facts:
Guitarist Dmitry Radzetskiy will debut as a concertmaster of a harmonica group: ‘...quasi una fantasia…’ has as many as five parts;
As rare as the name is, this concert actually features two Rimmas: the first violin is played by Rimma Benyumova, arriving from Berlin. Naturally, we didn’t invite her for the name;

Viktoria Vitrenko, who arrived from Cologne, has had a very difficult time with us thus far. First, we forgot to pick her up at the airport at midnight; then she discovered her hotel reservation was lost; later she could not rehearse properly due to a sudden intensification of construction works in Plivka. It’s all the more comical as we seem to have found a truly great Ukrainian singer in Cologne. Timbre, delivery, intonation — Viktoria has it all.

On October 27th Luigi Gaggero will comment on ‘Messages of the Late Miss R.V. Troussova’ at an open rehearsal at the House of Scientists at 7pm.  It is a very personal program for him, Luigi chose his arms (cimbalom!) after meeting Kurtág.

description: One day, a pupil of Le Corbusier came to Olivier Messiaen to study composition.

“I did something horrible which I should do with no other student, for I think one should study harmony and counterpoint. But this was a man so much out of the ordinary that I said... No, you are almost thirty, you have the good fortune of being Greek, of being an architect and having studied special mathematics. Take advantage of these things. Do them in your music” (Messiaen).
The student’s name was Yannis Xenakis. Seventy years later his music is still referred to as “an alien shard, glimmering in the heart of the West”.

There are only four days left before our final concert of the year, conceived as a radical celebration of Christmas. Joining the feast is Marino Formenti, who has just arrived from Vienna to solo in the Ukrainian premiere of Luciano Berio’s ‘points on the curve to find …’. Should you not yet know Marino Formenti, the Los Angeles Times offers a clear introduction: "in short, a phenomenon". The other soloist in the program is our own bass clarinetist, Artem Shestovsky – and we think he’s quite alright too.

There is also a notable guest in the composer department — Filip Rathé is here to partake in the premiere of his consistently changing work ‘Avec diamants extrêmes’. Rathé may not be from Antwerp, but they surely know a few things about diamonds in Ghent as well.
Radically and merrily yours, we intend to go into hibernation for a while right after this concert, so do not miss out.

name: Cosa Arcana e Stupenda
program: A collection of pieces written for the Ukho Ensemble by Claudio Ambrosini, Samuel Andreyev, Luca Antignani, Federico Costanza, François Couvreur, Konstantia Gourzi, Dmytro Pashinsky, Antonio Politano, Ruth Wiesenfeld, Philipp Rathé, and Ihor Zavhorodnii,
accompanied by premiere performances of works by Daan Jansens, Maxim Kolomiiets, Florian Mosch, and Alessandro Solbiati.
description: When we created the ensemble, we agreed with Luigi that every season, at least once or twice, we would talk about mummies; that three to four occasions would be reserved for conversations on death; and that we wouldn’t miss a single chance to touch on the nature of art and the enigma of life.

Following this agreement Gaggero asked fifteen European composers to comment on the famous phrase by Giacomo Leopardi from the “Dialogue Between Frederick Ruysch and His Mummies”. In it the dead admit that having found some certainty in death, they have not found grace. They remembered life only vaguely, but conceived it as “something mysterious and stunningly beautiful.” Being the minister of the “end of metaphysics”, Leopardi argued that although we are no longer able to cling to eternal truths (their stock has been depleted), art can still speak to a fascination with the secret of life and death, and can still turn confusion and shock into something beautiful.

Composers — Ambrosini, Wiesenfeld, Couvreur, Costanza, Andreyev, Rathé, Politano, Zavgorodnii, Gourzi, Pashinsky, and Antignani — were invited to tell something significant about the mysterious and beautiful in less than three minutes, relying on the full formation of the ensemble. The pieces turned out to be very diverse. Some strictly follow the logic of the text, others build upon the verse’s name, and a few inhabit only a single poetic image (e.g. the living flame in Rathé’s version, or the darkness of mystery in Wiesenfeld’s). As a whole, this collection of Cose Arcane primarily speaks about infinite variability of astonishment. This  impression is further deepened by four complimenting pieces — of Jansens, Kolomiiets, Mosch, and Solbiati, all written outside Leopardi’s call.
*It is now added to the agreement that we shall try to do something life-affirming at least once per season.

Samuel Andreyev – À propos du concert de la semaine dernière (2013) for piano and 7 instruments, 9’
description: There are many ways in which Soviet harps differ from their western counterparts: their strings are nylon, their pedals are tight, and their dimensions are modest. It is very difficult to rent a Soviet harp in Kyiv, let alone a Japanese or an American one: harps are very rare. So rare, in fact, that the entire Ukrainian Association of Harpists follows their every move. They do not land in the wrong hands.

Aida Aragoneses, an urgent Spanish replacement for our harpist, approaches her first Soviet instrument and carefully removes its cover, hand-sewn from pillowcases and fastened with dress buttons. “My darling!” Aida exclaims to the harp.

Stefano Gervasoni, just the way one might imagine him—very lively, with a soft, mocking face—drags a music stand and a cymbal cover up the red carpet to the second floor of the House of Scientists, where the ensemble rehearses. When he listens or talks, his mind visibly moves as easily and hyper-rhythmically as does his music. Upon our plea he brought along eight kilos of crotales from Paris, in Ukraine they are as rare as harps.

When several years ago Gervasoni decided to write about the Portuguese voice, he ended up composing a two-part cycle based on the transcriptions of Amalia Rodriguez’s Fado songs and the classical sonnets of Luís de Camões. The poetics of the latter has defined the Portuguese language to such an extent that Portugal Day is celebrated on June 10th every year to commemorate the day of his death in 1580.

We couldn't bring you Fado, but we did bring bass baritone Frank Wörner to sing a new version of “Dodici sonetti di Camões''. We also invited Eduardo Soares to play the guitar part, but there is a Ukrainian twist: some of his virtuoso passages will be played on domra by Svitlana Bilousova. After all, the Portuguese guitar belongs to the cittern, not the guitar family. Performed along the sonnets in our concert program are “Lilolela”, an earlier work by Gervanoni’s, and “À propos du concert de la semaine dernière” by Samuel Andreyev, a brilliant young composer whom Canada lost to France.

We are attempting the impossible: to release a Ukrainian ensemble on Winter&Winter. There are two conditions to the agreement: (1) the raw recording is our responsibility; (2) it needs to be alright. “Alright” is complicated. Among other things, in our case it implies that the entire staff of the National Dovzhenko Center where we record needs to leave work several hours early for an entire week, so that no rustle, no murmur on any of the Center's eight floors could distract Gaggero, Gervasoni, and Camões. Please come give them a listen.

program: Leonardo Marino – Concerto per pianoforte e ensemble (2017), 10’

description: We announced our composing masterclass in October; by December the jury selected ten applicants – each of them received a task to write a ten-minute concerto for ensemble and soloist. Through January and February concertos were developed under the gentle Skype-supportive guidance of Stefano Gervasoni and Samuel Andreyev. In early March an applicant from Taiwan left the race, leaving nine in.

It’s April now, and the participants have arrived in Kyiv. The majority are graduate and postgraduate students of well-reputed institutions – Paris and Milan conservatories, Oxford, the University of Chicago, and so on. UOC came through with notable kindness, having covered the road to us for the young Brazilian composer Rodrigo Bussad.

All through last week our guests were working in Plivka with the ensemble and teachers, discussing and polishing off the pieces they brought. Five compositions were selected for the final concert – aside the aforementioned Bussad, they belong to Julia Monducci, Nemanja Lakicevic, Lucas Tobiassen, and Leonardo Marino. Curiously, not all pieces fit with our usual aesthetics, hold you breath – there is even a metal concert among them! However, diverse as they are, all pieces showcase Ukho’s soloists, precisely how we imagined it back in October.

description: It won’t hurt to practice imaginary genealogy in the city of rifts – you are free to choose your own ground zero in it. Your roots could sit in any apartment, even the one you were invited to just once, on a Saturday afternoon in early autumn. As it happens, even one room of that apartment would do – ten soundproofed square meters, housing a grand piano and some books, an emerald chapel, hidden within a Soviet panel building on the street of Enthusiasts. A studio in which Valentyn Silvestrov has been listening for music for fifty years, and from which he has been writing consolation over the past quarter century.

Kyiv’s preeminent poet doesn’t write verses, he speaks them. Into a single sentence he packs “encumbrance,” “dangle,” “euphony,” and “particles that are negligibly small”. Four Grammy nominations, ten monograph albums on ECM, twelve encores at the Leningrad Philharmonic after the premiere of ‘Spectra’ — a star and a very private man, he was getting to his rehearsals with Kronos Quartet in Kyiv by subway.

On Saturday, September 30, Valentyn Silvestrov will turn 80. We are not interested in making eulogies, he is even less interested in receiving praise, but this truly calls for a celebration. Concerts dedicated to Silvestrov will be held in Kyiv all through September. We managed to accumulate ours in forty eight hours around his birthday. What shall we offer to his guests?

Firstly, an exhibition of audiovisual installations, with a small but exceptional documentary part found in the archives of Yevgen Gromov and Viktor Marushchenko, along with a number of new objects we commissioned from Katya Libkind, Stas Turina, Katya Lesiv, Sasha Yeltsin, Ivan Grabko, Alexei Shmurak, and Oleg Shpudeiko.
Secondly, a performance by Shmurak, Shpudeiko, and Mark Buloshnikov, which opens the exhibition and reflects on it.

Thirdly, a marathon of Silvestrov’s early works — his avant-garde and metaphorical music, very rarely performed today. The marathon is organized as a sequence of four concerts, with Ukho Ensemble’s performance closing the night. To make it happen, we have put together a special string quartet, brought Mario Caroli from Strasbourg to play the Mysterium for alto flute and six percussion groups, invited Vyacheslav Poprugin from Eindhoven to perform the Third Piano Sonata and Kitsch-music, and piloted Viktoria Vitrenko from Stuttgart to sing Forest music, Cantata, and Ode to the Nightingale. We have also booked Anthony Baryshevsky to play the Second Sonata, and ensured Yevgen Gromov provides for an hour of concert music along with many hours of fine tutorship for young Ukrainian musicians partaking in the program. Believe it or not, we have even managed to find four good violas to perform in Cantata! Also, we gave a story about a football match between a team of student composers and an all deaf team to Misha Buksha, our graphic artist, for him to get an idea of Kyiv avant-garde. Misha has responded to it by creating the most sporting poster.
* “Valentyn, how are you going to survive all this? — I plan to slalom! Which subway line is this Plivka?”
** It was in the late fifties, a 15:13 win for deafs.

name: Prof Bad Trip & Other Urban Legends
performers: Curators of the collective intervention: Sasha Andrusyk & Katya Libkind
program: Fausto Romitelli – Professor Bad Trip I-III (1998-2000), 45’
Tristan Murail – Legendes urbaines (2006) for twenty instruments, 32’
The Ukrainian premiere of these works is paired with Bad Trips (Everyday) — A Collective Exhibition with Mothers, an intervention curated by Sasha Andrusyk and Katya Libkind.
description: Saturday, November 11th. “My body's envelope floats freely around the furrow (how can it!?), an enormous balloon containing this little river, for this great furrow when I try to see my body at the same time is only a rivulet, but still lively, untamed, champagne and spitting cat.  An immense space between my body and the furrow, with the furrow running through the middle... so then I contain the furrow, except at its extremities which disappear in the distance, and yet it is myself, each of my instants, one after the other, flowing in its crystalline flux…“

Henri Michaux's book ‘Misérable Miracle’ was written in 1955 in the wake of his mescaline trips of the early fifties. Text aside the book included a set of forty-eight drawings; in the preface Michaux wrote that it was not the mescaline that he was exploring, but “the mediocrity of human nature.” A schoolboy from Namur, a medical dropout, a stoker on a merchant ship, a poet, a traveler, a hallucinating artist, Michaux allowed only one photograph of himself to be published during his lifetime. On it he is holding an ink pen over a heavily cluttered desk.

Fausto Romitelli did not live to the age at which Michaux first tried mescaline – Romitelli would have turned 54 this year. Among the many things he achieved in his short lifetime is his iconic cycle ‘Professor Bad Trip’ which builds on Michaux’s texts, Gianluca Lerici’s psychedelic comics, Gérard Grisey’s spectral compositions — and a solid curve of rock guitar. Romitelli's “obsessive, repetitive, visionary poetics'” emerged in this piece. ‘Professor Bad Trip’ takes 45 minutes, the concert’s remaining half hour is a walk through a more familiar landscape, with Tristan Murail’s ‘Légendes urbaines.’

In other news, every performance of Ukho Ensemble this season will feature a visual intervention – with Silvestrov we have been testing the waters (and waters are fine). This time the intervention will focus on our mothers and their bad trips, mundane rather than drug-induced.
Before entering the concert hall you will walk through a feast of nine dining tables, serving food and avocations. Exhibited for you will be a variety of objects produced in a pursuit of hobby.

A phenomenon of a persistent hobby interests us for its ambiguity. In its better form it means to comfort the individual pursuing it, by way of distracting him or her from everyday routine, relieving the existential panic and anxiety, taking away the feeling of immurement in all things usual. Another side to a hobby though is its hermeticism, automation, fixation.

Coincidentally panic attacks, anxiety, feeling of immurement, fixation are words that one often uses describing a bad trip. These are also words that we often hear from our mothers, describing their everyday life. One could argue that being a woman in her fifties in Ukraine is being in a continuous bad trip, hobbies often serving both as an exit and an entrance to it. We chose to speak of our mothers’ because it is the privilege of intimacy that lets one see this exit-entrance point cut open – and because our mothers agreed that we speak of them.
Collective exhibition of mothers
Participants:
mother of Katya S., mother of Ian S., mother of Katya L., mother of Sasha A., mother of Riba W., mother of Ola L., mother of Katya B., mother of Sasha P.
Curators:
Sasha А., Katya L.
*After everything was thought through and put together, this passage found itself in Michaux’s “Miserable Miracle”:

Mescaline can only furnish stunts: I see an enormous restaurant. Numerous stories, and people eating on all the balconies (yes there are balconies and with pillarets), thousands of tables, thousands of people eating, thousands of waiters in blue jackets. Funny idea! Dishes are served. Dishes are removed. Are served again. Are re-removed. No sooner is the dish served than the plate is taken away. No sooner is the plate set down than the dish is taken away. The speed is no longer even that of a comic gag, but of a metronome. It is not that of an alternating current either. Try to picture the details. These diners are like mannequins, the waiters too. No expression one can remember. No individuality in the movements either.

What possible explanation? This utterly idiotic spectacle is the translation of a prodigious mechanism. One must realise that Mescaline provokes the most violent sensations of hunger, here one instant, gone the next - sparks of hunger. Mescaline instantaneously images and realises sensations of ideas without the least participation of will and without any conscious desire. The silly gag is the result of this perfect automatic functioning.
What I hate most of all comes in the end - exhibitionism. Narcissism that speaks through clothes, table pleasures. Holiday atmosphere, colorful balconies, everything that is meant to be fun.
Pause.
Several pauses. Several colorful valleys. Another pause.

name: String Theory
program: Iannis Xenakis – Epicycle (1989) for cello and ensemble, 12’
Klaus Huber – In nomine - ricercare il nome…(1999) for ensemble, 8’
Michael Finnissy – Above Earth’s shadow (1985) for violin and ensemble, 20’
Brian Ferneyhough – Carceri d’invenzione I (1982), 11’
Claus-Steffen Mahnkopf –  Kammerkonzert (1995-1996) for piano & ensemble, 15’
Paired with a visual intervention by BLCK BOX
description: Strings (conjecture): suppose there are quantum strings — one-dimensional, thin, ultramicroscopic objects.
Vibration (conjecture): suppose quantum strings oscillate.
Poiesis (conjecture): suppose all elementary particles and their interactions occur as a result of these oscillations.
Experimental evidence: not available, poetics is available instead.
Poetics (hypothesis): vibrating quantum strings make no sound as we understand it, and yet at the most basic level all of the world is musical.
Front-pager:
We have a string program in which everything oscillates; also, it is the last concert this year.

While everything is buried in snowdrifts, our musicians are buried in both snowdrifts and unbearable music notation. With Xenakis, Huber, Ferneyhough, Finnissy, and Mahnkopf on the list, we are neck and neck with the music of New Complexity. Musicians groan, post heavily commented scores on Facebook, wonder aloud about Luigi —“can anything be done about this man?” — but persevere. Every Christmas Eve, in the season of miracles, we invite them to play the unplayable.

Juilliard graduate Rachel Koblyakov, our only foreign member besides the conductor, will solo in Michael Finnissy’s ‘Above Earth’s Shadow’ for violin and ensemble. Viktor Rekalo will perform the cello solo in Xenakis' ‘Epicycle’. Other ensemble pieces are ’In nomine – ricercare il nome….' by Klaus Huber and ‘Carceri d'Invenzione I’ by Brian Ferneyhough. Ferneyhough studied with Huber, Mahnkopf with Huber and Ferneyhough, Finnissy seems to be a prodigy. The concert aims to show the diversity of complexity’s sound: so fragile and hidden for Huber, and so obviously intense for his students.

Besides all this, there will also be a visual project. We were closely following the development of Kyiv’s rave and ambient scenes in the past few years, especially noting the ingenious light and projection work by the Black Box and the 1/16 collective – until finally inviting them to collaborate with the ensemble. The concept will present itself during the concert, the only thing one needs to know in advance is that the final movement of light  is programmed based on Rachel’s solo part in Finnissy’s piece. It will start when music quiets down. Light too is of the string nature.

name: REC#3: Ukho Ensemble Records Alessandro Solbiati
program: Alessandro Solbiati
Insieme (2017) for ensemble, 10’
Concerto (2016) for guitar solo and fifteen instruments, 24’
Sinfonia da camera (2006) for fifteen instruments, 17’30”
Neve (2017) for seventeen instruments, 4’
description: Microphone check!

In a week we are recording Ukho Ensemble’s third album in the large studio of the Ukrainian Radio Recording House. This is Alessandro Solbiati's monograph for EMA Vinci, and he has selected the pieces himself with a view of making one of the most important records of his life. Obviously, you should not be deprived of the opportunity to hear this program live in such a hall, which is why there will be a preview concert on Wednesday, January 24th, at 20.00. Over!
Footnotes:

Third album — an album we recorded third. This debut album releases in August 2018 on Winter&Winter and features music by Stefano Gervasoni that we put together in Plivka this April. To see the name of the ensemble listed in the same catalogue with Arditti Quartet, Exaudi, Ensemble Modern, Barbara Hannigan, Lorenzo Ghielmi, Fred Frith, Guy Klucevsek is truly thrilling. It would be impossible to believe had there not been evidence! The second recording, Toshio Hosokawa’s ‘Gardens’, was made this fall, and received a lot of praise from the composer. We are currently in talks with Kairos and Naxos, the results should be available by spring — but it is rather evident already that our chrysanthemum was recognized as this year’s prettiest far beyond Kyiv oblast.

** Alessandro Solbiati — an adornment of the Italian school, a student of Donatoni, and a successor of Maderna, who taught two generations of composers. You heard Solbiati last February (his ‘Neve’ shined in our ‘Cosa Arcana’ program) and at the very first concert of Luigi Gaggero organized by Ukho— his solo performance of 2013, which kickstarted our collaboration. Solbiati’s music originates in a deep understanding of tradition — from Gregorian chorales to counterpoint, then to avant-garde languages and further, to their (partial) overcoming. His artwork combines two different approaches: a keen interest in sound and an investigation of the timbral capabilities of instruments, on the one hand, and close attention to form, on the other. The first led to a significant expansion of the vocabulary for a number of instruments — guitar, button accordion, accordion, cymbals, Thai gongs etcetera (in our program this approach can be fully appreciated in the Guitar Concerto, written for Luigi Attademo). The second approach resulted in the fact that Solbiati’s compositions work as perfect mechanisms: complex, but coherent and crystal clear.

*** Such a hall — a hall designed in the mid-70s by the Leningrad Institute of Sound. It has a floating foundation, a ceiling of dispersive stalactites, and, apparently, Kyiv’s best acoustics, churches aside. All this time the hall was next door, in the backyards of the Klovska metro station, and we had no idea! It is under peaceful occupation from now on, as upon seeing it we immediately planned the ensemble’s recordings up to the tenth album.

description: This year it went like this: the first application arrived from Berkeley, the last one – from the Accademia Nazionale di Santa Cecilia; only one Ukrainian applicant got through to the top ten – but she was also the youngest of all, a richly talented 16-year old; each of the ten participants had to write a concert for an ensemble and several soloists, consulting the teachers, Luis Naon and Stefano Gervasoni, in the process. The process lasted for four months. A week ago the group finally reached Holosiyivska metro station, next to which they were meant to study from 10.30 to 21.30 daily, with teachers, ensemble, and Luigi Gaggero –  in turn and altogether.

On Saturday we are presenting you the results: five student compositions selected for the final concert, framed by the pieces of  the teachers. Come, this is the freshest and perhaps the most international of our servings.

program: Concert with live broadcast to the city nudist beach:
Luca Francesconi
Viaggiatore insonne for soprano and five instruments (1983), 9'
Mauro Lanza
Mare for voice, ensemble and toy instruments (2006), 14’
Stefano Gervasoni
Due poesie francesi di Rilke
for female voice, flute, clarinet, percussion, piano and string quartet (1995-1996), 9'
Luca Antignani
…ed il mare rispose in eco for voice, choir and ensemble (2003), 7'
Fausto Romitelli
Mediterraneo I - Les idoles du soleil for ensemble (1992-1993), 6'
Mediterraneo II - L’azur des déserts for voice and twelve musicians (1992 - 1993), 12'
subtitle: MARE
description: Attention! We are announcing our first teleconference.

The festival has enjoyed such success that the city’s water level has increased drastically and will continue to do so for the next couple of days. As a result, the musicians of Ukho Ensemble have barricaded themselves along with their instruments, and refuse to leave the dry comfort of Plivka. Naturally, this crashes our hopes to bring them to the central nudist beach of Kyiv for the concert. However, inspired by the example of the Berlin Philharmonic, we have decided to organize our own broadcast!

Today from 20.00 we will broadcast the ensemble from their Plivka stage to the beach, and the beach to Plivka’s main hall. The regular concert is paid, a concert on the large beach screen is free – you choose where to go or swim.

The program is a set of sea verses collected by our Genoese conductor. It opens with Francesconi's early work on the text by Sandro Penna; the vocal part is almost baroque. The singer looks at the sea, the sea is calm, “inside is a cry of joy”.

Mauro Lanza builds upon the text of Amelia Rosselli, who became famous in Italy thanks to Pier Paolo Pasolini. The sea manifests itself as a “huge beast-snail”, a “transparent flower”, and a “solid grave” at the same time. Half of the instruments are toys. Imagine using Grisey’s spectral language to tell Mad Hatter’s tale – this is what the music is like.

Gervasoni's piece, another early work, is based on Rilke's French poems, but they could have been written by Beckett. They disrupt the very ability to sing or speak, the wanderer is lost “on roads that lead nowhere”. The music every so often descends into delicate whisper.
Romitelli once said, “finding the right direction is impossible, the shipwreck is inevitable,” here leading us to the Mediterranean with the poem by Paul Valerie.
The concert’s finale is two South Italian pieces arranged by Antignani, following the example of Berio's folklore cycle. Both are sung in dialects (Apulian and Neapolitan), and “echoed by the sea”.

name: Architecture of Voice, Vol.3: Waters
performers: Alexey Shmurak, Oleh Shpudeiko,       with the participation of Vira Marchenko
program: A site-specific performance juxtaposing birdsong and human song within the setting of a hydrotherapy clinic
subtitle: The Nightingale (Соловей): Concert and Hydrotherapy Treatments
description: We meet today at three, at the Mud and Hydrotherapy Clinic at 34 Popudrenko Street. Getting there is easy enough: ten minutes by metro from Khreshchatyk, then a ten-minute walk from the station. The hydrotherapy clinic writes on its website that it was built in 1984, that it stores Kuyalnyk mud in its basements, and that more than 300,000 people have passed through its baths — including many distinguished individuals. A relict sanatorium, a magnet for Darnytsia ficuses and palms.

The common nightingale, on its Wikipedia page, claims that it is moisture-loving and that it reaches its greatest numbers in floodplain forests — but it was not taken to hydrotherapy for that reason alone. The title refers less to humidity than to Andersen’s fairy tale, with its opposition of the artificial and the natural.

The project that Alexey Shmurak and Oleh Shpudeiko conceived for Architecture of the Voice almost a year ago is musically built around two sources: birdsong (recorded and synthesised) and folk song (human, not avian). The authors work with synthesisers, voices, a sopilka, and various sound-engineering and compositional devices alongside authentic field recordings, and even enlist a singing doctor they met at the clinic.
Pearl baths, and the music written for them, can be taken after the concert, from 16:20, and are available for another week. Rain is no obstacle to them.

description: A key observation one is to make based on this poster is that our artist is already enjoying his summer vacation, sending us postcards that contain traces of salt and cypress. We are here, though, stuck on a tall dusty hill above the river hit by the annual algal bloom. Young conductors and conductresses are here too. They persevere, nevertheless.

On Sunday, in the last concert of the season, we shall listen - again -  to Xenakis’s ‘Epycicle', Huber’s ‘In nomine - ricercare il nome’, and Gervasoni’s ‘Animato’, presenting ‘Archeologia del telefono’ of Sciarrino for the first time. Lion hearts conducting the ensemble are Evan Meccarello (USA), Cristina Iscenco (Romania) and our own Dina Pisarenko. It is also rather unlikely that Luigi will resist partaking.

Tickets are sold at the entrance, mind that our next encounter is scheduled for the end of September. We do not know how many times exactly we shall have listened to the first release of the ensemble by then (a double figure?), but aye -  it went to print on Monday.

name: REC#4: Ukho Ensemble Records Samuel Andreyev
program: Samuel Andreyev
Le malheur adoucit les pierres (2002) for bass flute, English horn, bassoon — 5′
Nets Move Slowly, Yet (2006–07) for ensemble —10′
Événements quotidiens (2011) for ensemble — 7′
Night Division (2008–10) for alto flute, English horn, clarinet, trombone, piano, percussion, double bass — 10′
Iridescent Notation (2012–17), cantata for soprano and ensemble —  20′
description: Hello, this is the seventh season speaking. We open on Wednesday, September 26th, in the temple of acoustics and radio waves known as the Ukrainian Radio’s Recording House. Ukho Ensemble will arrive with the intention to record its fourth album — a monograph by Samuel Andreyev, a Canadian composer, poet, and performer. We have been preparing for it from the moment Andreyev, who came to teach at our composer workshop, heard Dina Pysarenko's piano solo in his composition and demanded that we cooperate immediately.

On the 26th of September we invite you to preview the recording live. The program includes an hour-long retrospective of Samuel’s music, from his first work, “Le malheur adoucit les pierres”, the only composition in his catalog written before his move to Paris in 2003, to the 20-minute cantata “Iridescent Notation”, completed eight months ago. The latter will be performed by Maren Schwier, a young soloist of the Staatstheater Mainz. The first composition is inspired by the automatic writing of the surrealists, the last is based on the texts of the modernist poet Tom Raworth. These pieces are separated by sixteen years of composing practice, and four other works in the concert program. Five out of six will be presented in Ukraine for the first time.

Andreyev works with a huge range of tones. “Nets Move Slowly, Yet” opens, for example, with a rare trio of bass flute, marimba, and double bass, to which other instruments gradually join, expanding eventually with French Horn solos. “Iridescent Notation” has an amazing percussion texture, its rainbow character suggests, in particular, that even flower pots are used. A tiny A-flat clarinet, a unicorn of the world of instruments, appears from the rustling thicket now and then – it was brought to Kyiv in July, so that Dmyro Pashinsky could get familiar with it.

Of course, there is also something in the program for those not particularly fond of rainbows: panic attacks in “Événements quotidiens”, dark colors in “Night Division”. Ukho stands its ground, do not worry

name: In a Swirl
program: Rebecca Saunders
Fury II (2009)
Concerto for solo double bass and ensemble, 13’
Gérard Grisey
Vortex temporum (1994-1996) for six musicians,  40’
description: “Music, impregnated by time, is invested with this violence of the sacred of which Bataille speaks; a violence silent and without language, that only sound and its’ becoming can possibly, and only for an instant, evoke and exorcise.”

The body of musical time, as it manifests to Gérard Grisey, consists of the skeleton, flesh, and skin. The skeleton is temporal structures used by composers to organize sounds — that is, durations, rhythms, and tempo. Hardly observable under the flesh of time, it gives the author the illusion of efficiency. The skeleton is chronometric, but can be perceived differently depending on how precisely the volumes and masses of musical flesh are distributed over it, and on how much of this flesh is pre-audible. The skin of the musical time interacts with the listener's perception of time, or to put it otherwise, the skin is what the listener will remember.

An unexpected sonic impulse forces us through a portion of time. Sounds that are perceived in the next moment — necessary to restore balance — do not have the same emotional value. The effect of the push interferes with the linear unfolding of time, and the time contracts. On the other hand, a series of predictable sound events facilitate perception, and even the smallest of them acquires significance. This is how time expands, for the acuity of auditory perception is inversely proportional to the accuracy of temporal perception.

On Saturday the ensemble will play “Vortex Temporum” by Gérard Grisey. The maelstrom of time or the funnel of time — we’ll see exactly what emerges. Grisey finished this piece two years before his death and thought of it as an illustration of his methods for working with musical time in the way he had described. It is a masterpiece, the last time we showed you something this important was in February.

In this concert the ensemble will also play the Ukrainian premiere of “Fury II” by Rebecca Saunders. It is known that Rebecca Saunders has more shades of red than did the Herat artisans; that she draws her sound objects by hand; that above all she is occupied with the materiality of sound; and that before writing something for an instrument, she takes several years to study it. “I don’t really have any intentions as to what the listener should experience. I write what I want to hear,” says Sauders. And what she wants to hear, in this case, is an extremely complex concerto for double bass, in which all instruments sound percussive. At the beginning of November “Fury II” will be performed by Klangforum Wien - but they don’t have Nazar Stets’ (touché, Klangforum).

performers: Maksym Kolomiiets, oboe, English horn, oboe d’amore

Concerto for cello and orchestra (1966), 15'
Chamber concerto for thirteen instruments (1969-1970), 19'
Concerto for piano and orchestra (1985-88), 22'
description: Ukho Ensemble Kyiv performs Ligeti's Piano concerto, Cello concerto and Chamber concerto with soloists Dina Pysarenko and Viktor Rekalo in light of an upcoming recording.

The Piano Concerto is an apogee of Ligeti’s complexity, being his most intricate score, as the composer himself has stated. In this work, his main task was to write a piece that is neither tonal nor atonal. This kind of endeavor has led far beyond building upon Western art music tradition — Ligeti borrowed a lot from various South Asian scales and used them simultaneously. That is why for a large part of the concert, the pianist plays predominantly the black keys with one hand and the white keys with the other. Ligeti’s rhythmic innovations borrow from diverse sources as well: from the Western medieval isorhythmic techniques, to music of Aka people from central Africa, to Heinz-Otto Peitgen’s chaos theory. The latter inspired the self-destructive rhythmic patterns of the fourth part of the concert. In short, Dina Pysarenko, Ukho Ensemble’s pianist, had to prepare for more than eight months, as Ligeti asks the impossible from the soloist.

Ligeti’s Cello concerto, an earlier work, demands that the soloist (in this case, Ukho Ensemble’s Viktor Rekalo), takes a different stance. The cello does not have to take part in a competition with the ensemble (a traditional feature of the concerto form), but rather functions as a hardly distinguishable sonic foundation for the entire work. The concert opens with a very long cello note, which insensibly grows out of silence. After a while, the ensemble joins in, obscuring the cello with static note clusters. The first part of the concert ends with a lonely cello dissolving in the void — the soloist, according to Ligeti’s instructions, plays “in a whisper.” The second part, however, gives space to some traditional virtuosity, involving a performance of a very dynamic tonal melody.

Chamber Concerto is written for thirteen instruments and every musician is implied to be a soloist. One of the most performed works of contemporary classical music, this composition is an embodiment of Ligeti’s micro-polyphonic style. A multitude of small instrumental parts are woven into a single fabric, almost no rhythm or harmony is perceptible. All parts are equally complex, which results in an extremely multi-layered texture. The listener can only follow the flow of the implied “currents” that merge into the polyphonic stream, which sometimes brings musical jokes — for example, a tiny melodic fragment in the spirit of late romantics.

name: The Artifice of Fire
program: Jörg Widmann, Liebeslied (2010) for eight musicians
Johannes Maria Staud, Incipit (2000) for alto trombone and ensemble
Karlheinz Stockhausen, Kontra-Punkte (1952-1953) for ten instruments
Leonardo Marino, L’artificio del fuoco (2019) for violin, cello and large ensemble *world premiere
description: Ukho Ensemble Kyiv closes its season with works by Karlheinz Stockhausen, Jörg Widmann, Johannes Maria Staud, and a premiere of Leonardo Marino’s double concerto for violin, cello and large ensemble.    /// – There is no future tense in the Sicilian dialect. When they make plans for tomorrow, they add “su campamu”, meaning “if we live”.
–  Oh sure, not surprising! We happen to know everything of Sicily and death..

–  But have you ever been to Agrigento? Have you seen, perhaps, how on the day of St Agatha in Catania a crowd of men runs up a steep hill after a boat with her statue? Do you know how slippery this hill is in the rain? You may have seen the Taormina Theatre in the afternoon, but not at night, when the stage backdrop is lit by the sea and lava spills. You like April better than August. There is neither fear, nor melancholy in ‘su campamu’, it only captures what still lasts today.

Two years ago, Leonardo Marino, a Sicilian, attended our composer school and shared its first prize with Brazilian Rodrigo Bussad. Following this we commissioned Marino to write a piece for the ensemble. Several days ago we received the score, published by Suvini Zerboni. It is an 18-minute Double Concerto for violin, cello and large ensemble, called 'L'artificio del fuoco’. In Italy, they call fireworks “artificial fire” or “fiery device.” Leonardo was born in Catania, a city that sits atop a huge fiery mouth: “Life in Milan would be almost bearable if not for the boring, empty horizon, with not a single Etna in it.”

The horizon in cities north of Milan — Berlin, Cologne, Innsbruck — also lack etnas, but is replete with composers Ukraine is yet to discover. Jorg Widmann, for example, is young and hugely successful. In 2018 he was the third most performed composer, only behind Arvo Pärt and John Williams. He studied clarinet in Juilliard, and composition with Henze and Rihm. Although Widmann employs a modern atonal dictionary, he envisions his music as, at the very least, to be a dialogue with the classicists and romantics — or even as an answer to the question of how historical musical forms and gestures could function nowadays. In describing his Piano Concerto he claims, for instance, that a particular minor second down means “lamentation, like in Monteverdi,” but immediately underlines that such idioms usually appear in his music unintentionally, as a product of musical intuition and listening experience. His music is very engaging to play (“I won’t use 12 alphorns just to stand out”), which may be one of the reasons for his industry-wide success.

Michael Jarrel’s student Johannes Maria Staud is a more abstract and interdisciplinary composer. In addition to musical education, he has philosophical training. No wonder his first large work, commissioned by the Berlin Philharmonic’s Simon Rattle, is called Apeiron. Staud is interested in the materiality of sound, hence the complex extended techniques. Even when he makes use of classical idioms (Mozart-esque expressivity in Segue, for instance), he does not build upon classical tradition — he dissects it. At the concert on June 9th, his ‘Incipit’ for trombone and orchestra will be played along with Widmann’s ‘Liebeslied’ and Marino’s premiere. Karlheinz Stockhausen’s Kontra-Punkte is meant as an antithesis for these three works — we shall see what will turn out to be artificial fire and what will present itself as a fiery device.
The Kyiv Conservatory’s main hall is arranged like a theatre, therefore we have our hands (or, rather, those of Katya Libkind and BlackBox) free to prepare the stage design for the performance.
* The Romans tore off the nipples of St Agate. Traditional funeral sweets are white hemispheres with red berries in the center.
* It’s +45 °C in Sicily in August.

name: Salvatore Sciarrino: between Arcimboldo and Nietzsche’s Last Man. Reflections on his music and our time
program: A lecture on listening, perception, and historical imagination in the music of Salvatore Sciarrino
description: In this lecture, Luigi Gaggero approaches the music of Salvatore Sciarrino as a practice of listening shaped by fragility, bodily perception, and the dissolution of musical subjectivity. Moving between the composite vision of Giuseppe Arcimboldo and Friedrich Nietzsche’s figure of the Last Man, the lecture considers Sciarrino’s work as music after drama, after transcendence, and after expressive certainty. Particular attention is given to the status of silence, micro-gesture, and suspended time in Sciarrino’s operas and instrumental works, as well as to the ethical demands such music places on performers and listeners in the present moment.

Mykola’s vest: an intervention by Mykola Babiychuk and Katya Libkind

Katya Libkind: “Mykola Babiychuk is 80 years old, and has spent a quarter of his life in the psycho-neurological facility in Pushcha Vodytsia north of Kyiv. On March 5, 2022 he was evacuated to the Pavliv Psychiatric Hospital in central Kyiv, along with the other 150 patients, as the northern part of the region was getting occupied by the Russian army. This is when we met - I came to volunteer to Pavliv Hospital together with Stanislav Turina.

The next day Mykola secretly showed me this vest, telling that he had been working on it for many years. It was a very dear, precious thing to him, he made it very clear. A day later, he gave it to me as present, not taking no for an answer. The vest had become my body armor, a bulletproof vest - I did not take it off all through March and April”.

name: Noisy Intro
program: A live electroacoustic performance using 2022 site recordings from Kyiv and the region
description: The opening piece of Kyiv Dispatch is a live electroacoustic performance built from the audio journal of Ukrainian sound artist and producer Ian Spektor, drawing on material recorded in Kyiv and the surrounding region throughout 2022. The work incorporates electric guitar and video, bringing together three artists whose earlier collaborations as residents of Plivka Art Centre and 2c1b grew out of shared interests in noise and musique concrète.

The axis of the piece lies between public and private listening: field recordings from emblematic sites such as Kyiv Central Army Hospital or the Kyiv–Przemyśl train coexist with intimate sonic fragments of family celebrations, solitary walks, and lengthy kitchen conversations. Spektor’s long-standing interest in early musicality—in children’s immediate, unmediated responses to sound—reappears here as an undercurrent

program: Maxim Kolomiiets, Vinyl Snails for flute, clarinet, vibraphone, piano, violin, cello – 7’

Gérard Grisey, Vortex temporum for flute, clarinet, piano, violin, viola, cello – 40’
description: Ukho Ensemble Kyiv is a group of seventeen musicians playing new music under the baton of Luigi Gaggero. It was founded in 2015 and its foundations were outwardly utopian: Ukho was ideated as an ensemble of Ukraine’s top soloists performing contemporary classical music without rehearsal time constraints, complimented by inventive visual and contextual narrative.

The ensemble was selected upon an open call out of over one hundred fifty applicants from all over the country. In the first several years its every concert was preceded by over 60 hours of rehearsals. Instead of a widespread structuralist approach to contemporary music, Ukho privileges a phenomenological one: its interpretations are not based on an abstract idea of time which needs to be calculated, but rather on the experience of empathic encounter between the interpreter, composition, and audience, which brings about the feeling of lived time.

Like all things Kyiv at present, Ukho Ensemble is living through a war — its musicians and audience separated by circumstances such as missile attacks, army service, family and friends blocked in the areas occupied by Russia etc.

Reuniting in Stuttgart for the first time this year, resurfacing a vortex, Ukho will perform a masterpiece of Gérard Grisey - his Vortex Temporum, “the only story of an arpeggio in space and time, below and beyond our auditory window”. Two of the three Ukrainian pieces in the program, “Vinyl snails” by Maxim Kolomiiets and “Joys of birds” by Igor Zavgorodnii, were written in 2016 in the memory of Pierre Boulez, based on the formation of his Dérive I. Alisa Zaika’s new piece, “he only dreamed of places now..”, which is to premiere in Stuttgart, was inspired, instead, by Toshio Hosokawa’s Drawing - one of Ukho’s signature bits.

description: Ten soundproofed square meters in a typical Soviet apartment on Enthusiasts Street, on Kyiv’s left bank—housing a grand piano, a sofa, and a multitude of books—make up Valentyn Silvestrov’s studio, where he has been listening closely to his music for over fifty years. The studio is emerald green: its walls and ceiling are covered in silk, giving it the appearance of a tabernacle, a chapel of sound. Fleeing Russian aggression, the composer was forced to leave it—possibly forever—in early March 2022.

Kyiv’s preeminent poet does not write verses; he speaks them. With four Grammy nominations, ten monograph albums on ECM, and twelve encores following the premiere of Spectra, Valentyn Silvestrov remains a deeply private figure, taking the subway to rehearsals with the Kronos Quartet. On September 30, 2022, he turned 85—an anniversary his home audience and friends could not share with him.

Featuring rarely performed instrumental works from the 1960s and 1970s, the program traces several major stylistic shifts in Silvestrov’s music: from early dodecaphonic compositions (Five Pieces for Piano, Projections, Elegy), through the intensified music theatre of Drama and the Second Piano Sonata, to the later metastyle of Kitsch-music. The program is presented by Evgeny Gromov, a pianist whose work has long been closely connected to the legacy of the Kyiv Avant-Garde, the group to which Silvestrov belonged.
In Projections, Gromov performs with Yevhen Ulianov and Lucas Gerin, in a rare instrumental constellation of harpsichord, vibraphone, and bells.

For the Violin Sonata from Drama, a work often cited as among the first in Ukrainian music to incorporate elements of happening, he performs alongside Rachel Koblyakov, concertmaster of the Ukho Ensemble. Pushed to the limits of texture and dynamics, the sonata shifts from sonoristic writing into instrumental theatre, concluding with silent gestures and a lit match in pursuit of ecstatic clarity
