Reuniting for the first time this year for the opening concert of AFEKT, Ukho Ensemble Kyiv will perform a masterpiece of Gerard Grisey – his “Vortex Temporum”, “the only story of an arpeggio in space and time, below and beyond our auditory window”. In this piece, showing how time works in music, and how its auditory perception could be programmed by particular musical means, Grisey literally places the listener in the funnel of time. Written by a Frenchman in mid 1990s it sheds light, among other things, onto one of the funnels that listeners and musicians in Ukraine experience now daily – that of time, running unbearably slow, intolerably quick, bending lives in its vortexes.

The “Rituals of Order” by Jānis Petraškevičs immerse us in a circular temporality and an infinite space: it’s impossible to describe the wonder inspired by the fascinating and mysterious sonic objects that magically appear and disappear within this temporal continuum.

The sounds of the French masters Grisey and Boulez resonate in Helena Tulve’s music, but they are transfigured by a poignant dreamlike lyricism of unsettling and seductive beauty, “At the night of decline”.

Maxim Kolomiiets, a composer and an oboist (Ukho’s own), wrote “Vinyl snails” in 2016 as a dedication to Pierre Boulez on his 90th birthday, based on the formation of “Derive I” – but tried refraining from making it very serious. The key audio image is listening to a record, familiar from childhood, with post-romantic and post-serial music at the same time. The record constantly stalls and goes astray at the beginning, giving the old-fashioned melodies a contemporary color. Ukho’s new vinyl (snail?) with Grisey’s “Vortex Temporum” will reach its listeners by the end of 2023, published by a new recording label – Kyiv Dispatch.

With an age gap of twenty years, Alisa Zaika and Maxim Kolomiiets belong to different generations of Ukrainian composers, both come from one teacher though – Alla Zagaykevych. The ensemble first worked with Alisa when she was only sixteen; her piece, “he only dreamt of places now…”, was written for Ukho in November 2022, and until now has only been performed once.

name: Lotto-cola, Mid-Devourers, and Other Stories in the Dark
performers: ujif_notfound (drones, synthesisers, amplified guitars), Alisa Haspyd (electronics), Yevhen Ulianov (percussion)
program: An electroacoustic performance based on texts by Halyna Tkachuk
description: Is there a middle that isn’t devoured by mid-devourers? What use is an accordion on a slippery winter path? How does one console a miserable liar, why are neglected chatterboxes dangerous, and—most importantly—who should a pleasant young person ask for a rozbezdziavka? We have finally turned our attention to things that truly matter: namely, how to make audio tales for children and their adults with an interest in new music.

Thirteen Stories in the Dark by Halyna Tkachuk is a collection of short absurdist texts that work with the genre of the children’s horror story so that it becomes comic. Darkness in Ukraine has changed irreversibly since Thirteen Stories in the Dark was published in 2016, though its eternal inhabitants—sabre-toothed monsters and other shaggy earthlings—persist. The performance is framed by this contrast between 2016 and 2024.

The electroacoustic work based on texts by Tkachuk is composed and premiered by ujif_notfound (drones, synthesisers, amplified guitars), with Yevhen Ulyanov (percussion) and Alisa Haspyd (electronics). Pre-recorded voices include pianist and new music advocate Yevhen Hromov; Nika Seleha of the Bozhychi ensemble of authentic singing; poet and curator Dmytro Kazakov; artist Olha Marusyn; and others. Additional voices by writer Katja Petrowskaja and singer Viktoriia Vitrenko are contributed from Berlin.
The performance forms the penultimate event (and final concert) of this edition of Kyiv Dispatch gets packed, a series of audiovisual performances connected to the launch of the Kyiv Dispatch label.
The project is funded by the Culture and Education Stabilisation Fund 2023 of the German Federal Foreign Office (Auswärtiges Amt) and the Goethe-Institut. Additional support is provided by GVL.

name: Death in June (accoustic memorial)
program: An audio installation accessed via geolocation, based on field recordings made in Kyiv, Chernihiv, and Volodarka (2022–23)
description: Death in June is an audio documentary by Ian Spektor, presented as the final work of this edition of Kyiv Dispatch gets packed. The 45-minute work consists of 53 tracks recorded in Kyiv, Chernihiv, and Volodarka during 2022–23. It is dedicated to four of Ian’s friends who were killed in the summer of 2023 near Bakhmut, in Orikhiv, and near Kupiansk: Hena Kolesnyk, Kostya Kuzin, Bohdan Mazurenko, and Anton Petrochko.

Commissioned by Ukho Music, the piece draws on material from Spektor’s long-running archive of on-site recordings. The work is twofold: the tracklist contains brief descriptions of each recorded situation and is equal in importance to the audio material itself.

A personal diary of loss, Death in June avoids stylizing reality or arranging it into contrasts. Children’s voices appear as they do in Ian’s daily life; the music of mourning processions enters as it does in Kyiv’s everyday soundscape. Almost all voices in Death in June, apart from those of priests, commanders, and hospital patients, belong to friends or neighbours. The story centres on four heroes who, with one exception, are never heard directly.

Despite the abundance of what might be described as “ambience”—open urban spaces, homes, concert halls, hospitals, churches—Death in June is not an ambient work. No musical elements were added; about half of the material consists of fragments of conversation, overheard and later remembered — sometimes clear, often submerged in the noise of other memories.

Tracks overlap in time: some last only a few seconds, others up to thirty minutes, with three or four situations often audible simultaneously. Some recordings appear only once, while others periodically emerge from near-inaudibility into the foreground. The tracklist, which briefly describes each recorded situation, marks only the first occurrence of every track.

Spektor’s work will later be released on vinyl by Kyiv Dispatch, as a cast of the sonic and lived environment in which Ukho exists, and as an inevitable context for the label’s subsequent releases. For now, it is made available as an acoustic memorial. To listen, one must reach Lvivska Square in Kyiv, enable geolocation, and visit deathinjune.kyivdispat.ch.

Movement is not restricted, but the work is best experienced on headphones, while walking along the nearby slopes. A significant part of the recordings was made within the triangle formed by Zoloti Vorota, St Michael’s, and Lvivska Square.
The piece contains explicit language.

When Ian was obtaining permission from relatives to publish the work, Nelia, Bohdan’s wife, responded with a screenshot from an earlier exchange with her husband. He had written that he had just been to an Ukho Ensemble’s concert and suggested watching a recording. The file was titled Bacchanalia.
For both the author and the publishers, Death in June remains one of the most personal works made public.
The project is funded by the Culture and Education Stabilisation Fund 2023 of the German Federal Foreign Office (Auswärtiges Amt) and the Goethe-Institut. Additional support is provided by GVL.

name: Listening to absence: Sound archives of (non-) empty rooms
performers: Oleh Shpudeiko (Heinali), Kachna Baraniewicz, Maryna Osnach, Ian Spektor, Evgeny Gromov, Ihor Zavhorodnii, Veronika Selega, Olha Atamanchuk, Georgiy Potopalsky, Alexey Shmurak, Dmytro Radzetsky, Tymish Melnyk, Oleksandr Kariev, Ruslan Solovey, Displaysman, Sasha Andrusyk, Anton Zhukov [installation authors and contributing artists]
program: A series of audiovisual installations for DOCU/SYNTHESIS 2024, drawing on field recordings from Kyiv and its surroundings (2011–2024)
description: The war continues, and we continue to witness it, to register it. Given the millions of phones and phone cameras in existence, this is probably the most widely documented war in history. But a phone is a sound recorder, too, and the war is also an intense sound event, a space of transgressive sound. Long, close listening to the sound of mortal danger—the sound that signals someone’s death, the sound of grief—conditions our hearing just as much as listening to a lack of sound does: we are surrounded by a mass of silenced sound spaces, empty rooms.

This audio installation programme brings together works based on field recordings made in Kyiv and nearby locations between 2011 and 2024 and consists of three parts, which differ both in the timeframes of the recordings—Kyiv before the full-scale invasion, Kyiv after it, and Kyiv in a moment of transition—and in the ways the recorded material is treated.
Kyiv Eternal (2023)
Audiovisual installation by Oleh Shpudeiko (Heinali) Video by Maryna Osnach and Kachna Baraniewicz Mapping by Illia Kovalenko (ctrl-i)

The first part addresses the pre-2022 city, now irreversibly altered. Kyiv Eternal draws on field recordings collected between 2011 and 2021 and treats them as points of entry into emotional states remembered and revisited. These fragments—tram rides in the Vidradnyi district, winter in Shuliavka, minibus drivers calling out to passengers, birds in the Botanical Garden, night walks through the city, grocery stores, the Landscape Alley, metro journeys—are developed into cyclical structures of memory through ambient compositional techniques.
Sound functions here as a means of movement through the city, using all available modes of transportation, both literal and associative.
The installation incorporates video works by Maryna Osnach and Kachna Baraniewicz, assembled from private video archives from the same period and mapped by Illia Kovalenko.
Death in June (2024)
Audio documentary by Ian Spektor

The second part focuses on Kyiv after the full-scale invasion. Death in June is a 45-minute audio documentary by Ian Spektor, composed of 53 overlapping field recordings made in Kyiv, Chernihiv, and Volodarka during 2022–2023. Dedicated to four of the artist’s friends who were killed in the summer of 2023, the work functions as a private diary of loss and a sonic imprint of the city during that period. Built entirely from documentary material, it avoids aestheticization: children’s voices, mourning processions, and everyday sound environments appear as they occur in daily life. Tracks vary in length and overlap, with the accompanying tracklist marking only the first occurrence of each sound event, serving as a structural index rather than a playback guide.

Spektor explores music as it appears in the urban vernacular—accidental, amateur, and liminal, as well as ritual and everyday—and treats documentary sound as a form of soundscaping memory, organizing audio fragments into coherent narratives with minimal intervention beyond editing
Archive of Home Recordings (Spring 2022)

The third part of the programme presents a compact archive of home music recordings made by Kyiv residents in the spring of 2022 and collected by the Ukho agency. These recordings document moments of private music-making during the first months of the full-scale invasion: fragments captured on phones in apartments, studios, borrowed spaces, shelters, or temporary places of refuge.

The archive consists of performances, sketches, improvisations, and first ideas, often recorded without the intention of publication. These are intimate, private exchanges between individuals and their instruments, with contributions from Evgeny Hromov, Ihor Zavhorodnii, Alexey Shmurak, Olha Atamanchuk, Dmytro Radzetskyi, Tymish Melnyk, Oleksandr Karyev, Nika Seleha, displaysman, Ruslan Solovey, Sasha Andrusyk, Georgiy Potopalskyi, and Anton Zhukov.
Curated by Sasha Andrusyk

name: Kyiv Dispatch: label launch and first release — Liebestod by Alexey Shmurak and Oleh Shpudeiko
place: The Historic Cistern section of the Liquor and Spirits factory (Kudriavka)
program: A full album listening of Liebestod with introductory remarks, alongside a quadraphonic audio installation by Alexey Shmurak developed from the work’s material
description: Liebestod is the first record by the Kyiv duo Alexey Shmurak and Oleh Shpudeiko (recording solo as Heinali).
Written throughout 2021–2022, the album unfolds as a surreal journey through a kaleidoscope of song and dance tropes, poetic worlds, and languages—a meta-verse of a work.

The title refers to Richard Wagner’s Tristan und Isolde and its notion of love–death: an ambivalent mood between ecstatic delight and the anticipation of death. The heroine of Tristan und Isolde lives through hours of unsettling discord before breaking into a final, rapturous union with her dead beloved and lifelessly sinking onto Tristan’s body in a “vast wave of the world’s breath.”

In Shmurak and Shpudeiko’s interpretation, love–death represents music that speaks to the end of a small person’s intimate story while heralding an encounter with a macro-narrative—history. The fragility of relationships and lives, uncertainty about the safety of loved ones, separation, nostalgia, the yearning for closeness, the looming terror of what is to come, and the acceptance of its enormity—these are the themes of this album. Texts by Ivan Mazepa, Volodymyr Svidzinsky, Inger Christensen, and John Keats are woven into a conversation between a vulnerable, self-deprecating voice and electronics that gradually exert increasing pressure on the voice before inundating the entire space.
The album is presented at ABO through a full listening session accompanied by introductory remarks and a playlist designed for the occasion.

Alongside this presentation, a quadraphonic audio installation by Alexey Shmurak translates the material of Liebestod and its multiple drafts into a spatial form, installed in the historic cistern of the Liquor and Spirits Factory in Kudriavka. A person humming seventeenth-century intimate lyric poetry over a piano at home, a giant metronome concealed among the columns, an incongruous bossa nova, and almost-sirens that never become sirens—elements that register as unstable points of perception.

name: Limbo by Viktoriia Vitrenko: a performative lecture & vinyl presentation
place: Jam Factory,  Art Area DK Kharkiv, Odesa National Fine Art Museum, Dnipro Centre for Contemporary Culture, Vere Music Hub
performers: Viktoriia Vitrenko (lecture and bits of performance)
program: Viktoriia Vitrenko presents her Limbo album, published by Kyiv Dispatch, featuring works by Agata Zubel, Alla Zagaykevych, Ying Wang, Sven-Ingo Koch, and Maxim Shalygin
description: As part of the release of LIMBO, a double vinyl published by Kyiv Dispatch, Viktoriia Vitrenko presents a series of performative lectures combining live performance, spoken commentary, and audiovisual material. The album brings together vocal cycles by Agata Zubel, Alla Zagaykevych, Ying Wang, and Sven-Ingo Koch, written in 2021, alongside fragments from a cycle by Maxim Shalygin (2009–2010), and is dedicated to flutist and dissident Maria Kalesnikava, who is serving an eleven-year prison sentence in Belarus.

The lectures take place in Lviv, Kharkiv, Odesa, Dnipro, and Kyiv and focus on the structure, vocal writing, and performative demands of the works included in the LIMBO program. During the events, Vitrenko sings and accompanies herself at the piano while discussing compositional approaches and vocal techniques such as mormorando, Sprechgesang, traditional Ukrainian singing, bel canto, and extended techniques, as well as the expressive states of waiting, suspension, and uncertainty that shape the album. The lectures also incorporate excerpts from an audiovisual installation developed in collaboration with Studio Radar. In Lviv and Kyiv, Alla Zagaykevych joins Vitrenko for the events.

Limbo was recorded in August 2022 in Muzychi with recording engineer Herman Safonov; sound post-production was carried out by Rashad Becker, Studio Clunk. The vinyl features photography by Elena Subach and design by Dasha Podoltseva; the release was produced by Sasha Andrusyk and Viktoriia Vitrenko.
The performative lecture series is realized in collaboration with Goethe-Institut Ukraine.

name: Limbo: Pop-up by Elena Subach and Presentation of the Vinyl
program: An exhibition by Elena Subach presenting photographs from the LIMBO vinyl alongside related works on liminality
description: An exhibition by Elena Subach at The Naked Room gallery presents photographs made for Viktoriia Vitrenko’s Limbo vinyl alongside additional images addressing liminal states. Created as the visual counterpart to the Limbo release, Subach’s work uses direct flash and collage to place figures and scenes in suspended, threshold-like situations. The exhibition accompanies the Limbo double-vinyl release on Kyiv Dispatch and forms part of the album’s presentation program

name: Small Song
place: Old Academic Building of Kyiv Mohyla Academy
program: Opening performance of Jannis Kounellis, Untitled (1997–2005): cante de fiesta in dialogue with the teachings of Rabbi Nachman of Breslov
description: On June 15, Faktura 10 opens the exhibition Untitled (1997/2025) by Jannis Kounellis, curated by Marta Kuzma, in the Old Academic Building of the Kyiv-Mohyla Academy. The exhibition reconstructs a project by Kounellis—one of the founders of arte povera—originally created during his lifetime in 1997 for the Soros Center for Contemporary Art. It also marks Kuzma’s first curatorial project in Kyiv in more than twenty-five years.
We are pleased to take part in the opening with a performance by Isaac de Judea and Olya Ostroverkh, accompanied by Pavlo Kosenko (guitar) and Oleksiy Khokhun (cajón), curated by Sasha Andrusyk.

Small Song takes the form of a shared practice of cante de fiesta, the festive branch of flamenco—a repertoire historically grouped under the term cante chico (“small song”). The choice of form is not aesthetic but liturgical: according to the teachings of Rabbi Nachman of Breslov, mid-June marks a period of the “obligation of joy,” during which it is to be cultivated and transmitted. Isaac de Judea, born in Granada and now based in Uman—the site of Rabbi Nachman’s grave—follows Breslov Hasidic teachings both in daily life and in artistic practice.

In this performance, de Judea works within cante de fiesta, historically distinguished from cante jondo by its lighter, less solemn character. The older term chico does not imply reduced technical complexity, but refers to an informal, domestic mode of performance. Many of the most personal and versatile flamenco expressions take place within this register. Most of the lyrics sung by Isaac are authored or adapted by him, following a longstanding flamenco tradition in which singers compose their own verses. These include tangos de Granada, tangos de Kyiv, tangos de Uman, and alegrías referencing Jewish histories in both Cádiz and Uman.

Flamenco emerges from histories of poverty, resistance, and survival and, like arte povera, engages austerity as a means of transformation. Both practices emphasize minimal means and reject the superfluous in favor of essential materials and processes. Flamenco, particularly in its traditional settings, foregrounds voice, breath, compás, and bodily presence. Although it can involve spectacle, it remains structurally grounded in constraint, repetition, and transmission. This performance draws on that shared ground.
Here, joy is not an effect but a discipline, enacted as injunction. As Rabbi Nachman taught:
“If you believe you can damage, then believe you can repair.”

name: Prolegomena to an Opera: Songs for Kyiv Mohyla Academy
place: Old Academic Building of Kyiv Mohyla Academy
program: A premiere of Maxim Kolomiiets' chamber song cycle set to the texts written or collected by Sasha Andrusyk
description: Composed by Maxim Kolomiiets, the chamber cycle turns to the history of the Kyiv-Mohyla Academy, where music—though absent from the formal curriculum—played an important role alongside theology, philosophy, and the classical languages. Drawing on Ivan Kuzminsky’s research into eighteenth-century musical practices, the libretto by Sasha Andrusyk combines historical reconstruction with an imaginative portrayal of the Academy’s milieu. The piece brings together a series of “situations” in which students of the Kyiv-Mohyla Academy sang or might have sung in the eighteenth century—some based on authentic texts and melodies (begging for alms, funerals), some on recontextualized original texts (parties, recreations), and some entirely stylized or speculative (liturgy, mammulae, disputation). Across the cycle’s seven parts, song appears as a living mode of instruction, rhetorical practice, and collective action. The material is performed by an ensemble of nine instruments and three vocalists: Denys Sahirov, Ruslan Kirsh, and Kostiantyn Lenchyk. Katya Libkind’s scenography engages with the architectural memory of the Academy, while Stanislav Turina’s alms bowls, incorporated into the staging, evoke the tradition of student begging.

The project was commissioned by “Faktura 10,” a key initiative of Ribbon International. It unfolds in dialogue with Jannis Kounellis’s Untitled, 1997/2025, first realized nearly three decades ago and newly reinstalled in June 2025 on the ground floor of the Old Academic Building of the Kyiv-Mohyla Academy.

name: Listening to Gérard Grisey’s Vortex Temporum
program: A lecture by Alexey Shmurak on Gérard Grisey’s Vortex Temporum, marking the Kyiv Dispatch vinyl release by Ukho Ensemble Kyiv
description: Gérard Grisey’s Vortex Temporum, performed by Ukho Ensemble Kyiv under the direction of Luigi Gaggero, is released on vinyl by Kyiv Dispatch, with artwork by Dariia Kuzmych and layout by Dasha Podoltseva. It is the label’s third release.
The release is marked by an album presentation and a lecture by composer Alexey Shmurak at Vere Music Hub, devoted to Grisey’s Vortex Temporum.

Vortex Temporum is a landmark piece from Gérard Grisey’s late period: emotionally intense, vivid, and at times virtuosic, yet permeated by themes of slowing down, dying, and cooling. The work combines spectralist techniques—requiring highly precise, non-traditional control of time and pitch—with the conventions of a chamber ensemble, including a piano tuned according to standard practice. This combination alone presents a significant technical and compositional challenge. Yet within these tensions, Grisey also introduces elements of groove reminiscent of jazz, as well as resonances that suggest folkloric or romantic expressivity. In his lecture, Alexey Shmurak discusses the logic of these juxtapositions, the compositional principles of Vortex Temporum, and Grisey’s conception of musical time.

The album was recorded in late 2022 in Stuttgart, during Ukho Ensemble Kyiv’s only gathering in the year of the full-scale invasion. The digital release, issued in May 2025, received strong reviews in The Guardian, Pizzicato, ResMusica, Luminous Dash, Neue Musikzeitung, Ruch Muzyczny, Sequenza21, and was selected as an Album of the Month in Bandcamp’s contemporary classical category. Prepared by Kyiv Dispatch, this edition marks the first vinyl release of Vortex Temporum.
